methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $178M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $1.0B - $2.0B.

private.name: Hadrian
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series C), post-money $1.6B

private.name: Nimble Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B

private.name: Bright Machines
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series C)

data.narrative:
Seurat Technologies is a Series C robotics and manufacturing company that has raised $178M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparison universe of nine private peers and one public comparable (277810.KQ), with particular reference to same-stage companies including Hadrian ($1.6B, Series C) and Nimble Robotics ($1.0B, Series C).

Two methods triangulate the valuation. Stage-progression analysis applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical of Series C advancement, yielding $303M based on total capital raised as a proxy. Peer-median analysis across five robotics companies at Series B through Series D stages—Exotec ($2.0B), Bedrock Robotics ($1.8B), Dexterity ($1.6B), Hadrian, and Nimble Robotics—produces a median valuation of $1.6B, with range of $1.0B to $2.0B. The stage-progression method reflects capital deployed; the peer-median method reflects market pricing for comparable risk and stage profiles.

The two-method triangulation supports a range of $257M (low) to $1.9B (high), with a median of $976M. The wide dispersion reflects material uncertainty around relative maturity and market traction versus the peer set. Stage-progression alone implies considerably lower valuation than peer comps, suggesting either that Seurat commands a discount to peer valuations or that peer multiples embed favorable market conditions in frontier robotics. Resolution of this gap requires deeper diligence on revenue, growth, and unit economics relative to named peers.
